title: Pharmacology Part A: GI & Pain Management
description: GI Meds (H2, PPIs, Laxatives) & Pain (NSAIDs, Opioids, Adjuvants)

questions.text: How do H2 Receptor Antagonists (e.g., Famotidine) work?
options: Block Histamine 2 receptors on parietal cells, 'turning down the volume' on acid production
questions.explanation: H2 blockers stop the histamine signal to produce acid, effectively reducing production.

questions.text: Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs) like Omeprazole are more potent because they:
options: Directly block the enzyme that pumps acid, 'shutting off the power'
questions.explanation: PPIs irreversibly block the proton pump itself, offering the strongest acid suppression.

questions.text: A long-term risk of chronic PPI use (e.g., Omeprazole) is:
options: Increased risk of fractures (due to poor calcium absorption)
questions.explanation: Acid is needed to absorb calcium. Long-term suppression weakens bones.

questions.text: Magnesium-based antacids (e.g., Milk of Magnesia) often cause:
questions.explanation: Magnesium moves things (Diarrhea). Aluminum/Calcium constipate. That's why they are often combined.

questions.text: Which patient population must be careful with Magnesium-based antacids?
options: Renal (Kidney) Failure patients (risk of magnesium toxicity)
questions.explanation: Kidneys excrete magnesium. If kidneys fail, Mg accumulates to toxic levels.

questions.text: Metoclopramide (Reglan) is used for GERD and Gastroparesis because it:
options: Promotes gastric motility (empties the stomach faster)
questions.explanation: It's a pro-kinetic agent. It pushes food through the GI tract.

questions.text: Stimulant Laxatives (e.g., Dulcolax/Bisacodyl) work by:
options: Irritating the intestinal wall to trigger contractions (peristalsis)
questions.explanation: They 'stimulate' the nerves in the gut wall. Effective but can cause dependence.

questions.text: According to the WHO Pain Ladder, Step 1 for mild pain involves:
questions.explanation: Start low: Tylenol or Motrin first.

questions.text: Acetaminophen (Tylenol) acts on pain but lacks this property found in NSAIDs:
questions.explanation: Acetaminophen is analgesic and antipyretic, but NOT anti-inflammatory.

questions.text: COX-1 enzymes (blocked by NSAIDs) are responsible for protecting the stomach lining. Blocking them causes:
questions.explanation: The 'good' COX-1 protects the gut. NSAIDs block it, stripping that protection.

questions.text: Long-term use of NSAIDs (blocking COX-2) is associated with an increased risk of:
questions.explanation: Chronic COX-2 inhibition upsets the clotting balance, increasing heart risks.

questions.text: The major deadly side effect of Opioids (e.g., Morphine) is:
questions.explanation: Opioids tell the brainstem to stop caring about CO2, leading to stopped breathing.

questions.text: Fentanyl is a potent opioid that, when given IV/Mucosal, has an onset of:
questions.explanation: Fentanyl is fast-on, fast-off (unless in a patch).

questions.text: Methadone is used for chronic pain and detox primarily because of its:
questions.explanation: Its long half-life prevents the daily up-and-down withdrawal cycle.

questions.text: What is 'Pseudo-addiction'?
options: Drug-seeking behavior caused by UNDERTREATMENT of pain
questions.explanation: The patient looks like an addict (demanding meds), but they are actually just in pain. Treat the pain, and the behavior stops.

questions.text: Adjuvant medications for Neuropathic Pain include:
questions.explanation: Nerve pain doesn't respond well to opioids. Seizure and depression meds calm the nerves effectively.

questions.text: EMLA cream (local anesthetic) must be applied:
questions.explanation: Topical anesthetics need time to penetrate the skin to work.

questions.text: Which drug prevents Platelet Aggregation (blood thinning) by inhibiting COX-1?
questions.explanation: Aspirin permanently disables platelets for their life (7 days).

questions.text: Sucralfate works for ulcers by:
options: Creating a physical protective barrier (coating) over the ulcer
questions.explanation: It's a 'band-aid' for the stomach.

questions.text: The antidote for Opioid Overdose is:
questions.explanation: Naloxone bumps the opioid off the receptor immediately.

questions.text: Which laxative is safest for daily use?
questions.explanation: Bulk formers just add fiber and water, mimicking natural diet.

questions.text: Stool Softeners (Docusate) work by:
options: Lowering surface tension to let water penetrate the stool
questions.explanation: They make the stool 'soggy' (softer) but don't force it out.

questions.text: Severe constipation from Opioids is due to:
questions.explanation: Opioids bind to the gut receptors and stop movement.

questions.text: What is the maximum daily dose of Acetaminophen for healthy adults?
questions.explanation: 4g is the limit. Beyond that, the liver enzyme gets saturated and toxic metabolites form.

questions.text: Misoprostol is often given with NSAIDs to:
questions.explanation: Since NSAIDs remove protective prostaglandins, Misoprostol puts them back.

questions.text: Which pain type is 'Spontaneous Breakthrough Pain'?
options: Unpredictable spikes in pain not related to activity
questions.explanation: It happens out of nowhere, unlike Incident pain (movement) or End-of-Dose failure.

questions.text: Loperamide (Imodium) is an antidiarrheal that is structurally related to:
options: Opioids (but does not cross blood-brain barrier)
questions.explanation: It uses the 'constipating' side effect of opioids without the high.

questions.text: Ondansetron (Zofran) works for nausea by blocking:
questions.explanation: It blocks the signal from the gut/brain to vomit.

questions.text: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) works by inhibiting HMG-CoA Reductase, which:
questions.explanation: It stops the factory line for cholesterol production.

questions.text: A common side effect of Niacin (Antilipidemic) is:
questions.explanation: Niacin causes vasodilation in the skin -> Flushing. Aspirin can help reduce this.
